content:
Great question! The tradition of Time magazine selecting a "Person of the Year" (originally "Man of the Year") started in 1927. The designation is given annually to the person, group, idea, or object that, in Time’s words, "for better or for worse... has done the most to influence the events of the year."

**Why have all U.S. presidents been selected?**

Since Time started this tradition, every elected U.S. president has been named Person of the Year at least once—sometimes more than once, and sometimes even before taking office (as "President-Elect"). This is because the U.S. president is generally seen as one of the most powerful and influential figures in the world, especially during their term.

**A few notable points:**
- **Even losing candidates or presidents-elect have sometimes been chosen:** For instance, Barack Obama was named in both 2008 (as president-elect) and 2012 (after his re-election).
- **Not always for positive reasons:** The title is not necessarily an honor; it reflects influence, for better or worse. For example, Richard Nixon was Person of the Year in 1971 and again (shared) in 1972, amid the Watergate scandal.
- **No president since 1927 has missed out:** Every president since Calvin Coolidge (who was in office when the tradition started, but was never selected) has been Time’s Person of the Year during their tenure.
- **Before 1927:** Presidents before the tradition began obviously were not eligible.

**Why is this the case?**
The American presidency is a highly visible, powerful, and influential office. The president’s actions, decisions, and policies often have global consequences, making them an obvious and frequent choice for Person of the Year.

**In summary:**  
All U.S. presidents since Time began the Person of the Year tradition in 1927 have been selected at least once, reflecting the office
